fact: December 11 is exactly 10 days before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which means the sun is getting lazier and lazier each day!
In Antarctica, December 11 falls during their summer, so penguins are having beach parties while you're bundling up for winter!
This date marks the 345th day of the year, leaving only 20 days left - you could count them all on your fingers and toes!
Arctic foxes are typically growing their fluffiest white winter coats right around this time, making them look like walking snowballs with eyes!
In northern countries, the sun might only peek above the horizon for 6 hours or less on December 11, making it feel like one long, cozy evening!
Evergreen trees like pine and spruce are slowing down their sap flow around this time to avoid freezing solid like tree popsicles!
December 11 falls in the middle of Sagittarius season, when the zodiac archer is supposedly aiming arrows at the approaching winter!
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are usually in their deepest sleep phase around this date - they're basically professional nappers right now!
In places like Alaska, the aurora borealis (northern lights) often put on their best light shows because the nights are super long and dark!
December 11 is prime time for ice crystals to form intricate patterns on windows in cold climates - nature's own frozen artwork that melts by morning!
Owls are becoming extra chatty around this date in northern regions because it's peak hunting season for them, with long nights giving them more time to hoot and hunt!
The moon phase on December 11 changes every year, but when it's full around this time, it stays visible for nearly 15 hours in northern regions!
